title: Birth of Guru Nanak Dev Ji
body: Guru Nanak is born in Talwandi (now Nankana Sahib, Pakistan) to Mehta Kalu and Mata Tripta. The accounts of his childhood describe a boy who questioned ritual without meaning and treated no human being as beneath him. His sister Bebe Nanaki is remembered as the first to recognize what he was.

title: The Revelation at the Kali Bein
body: At Sultanpur Lodhi, Nanak disappears into the river Bein for three days. He emerges with the words that begin the faith: 'There is no Hindu, there is no Musalman' — meaning that the labels people kill over dissolve before the One. He gives away his possessions and sets out to teach.

title: The Udasis — Four Great Journeys
body: With the Muslim musician Bhai Mardana at his side, Guru Nanak travels by foot across South and Central Asia — east to Assam, south to Sri Lanka, north into the Himalayas, west to Mecca and Baghdad. At every stop he debates priests, qazis, and yogis with the same message: truth is high, but higher still is truthful living.

title: The Babarvani — Witness to Invasion
body: Guru Nanak witnesses Babur's sack of Saidpur and is briefly imprisoned. His response is the Babarvani hymns, preserved in the Guru Granth Sahib — an unflinching protest against the violence of empire and the suffering of ordinary people. It establishes a precedent: the faith will speak against power, in public, in writing.

title: Kartarpur — The First Community
body: Guru Nanak settles at Kartarpur on the river Ravi and farms his own fields. The pattern of Sikh life is set here: kirat karo (honest work), naam japo (remembrance), vand chhako (share what you have). All sit in one line to eat, whatever their caste — the langar begins as a quiet, radical equality.

title: Guru Angad and the Gurmukhi Script
body: Before his passing, Guru Nanak tests his followers and chooses Bhai Lehna — renamed Angad, 'of my own limb' — over his own sons. Guru Angad standardizes the Gurmukhi script, putting scripture into the people's hands rather than a priestly language. His wife Mata Khivi runs the langar so generously that she is praised by name in the Guru Granth Sahib.

title: Guru Amar Das — Equality Made Institutional
body: Guru Amar Das, who became a Sikh in old age, rules that no one — emperor or beggar — may see him without first eating in the langar, seated on the floor with everyone else. He condemns sati and purdah, appoints women among his preachers, establishes the manji teaching districts, and composes Anand Sahib, the song of bliss still sung at every Sikh ceremony.

title: The Founding of Amritsar
body: Guru Ram Das founds Ramdaspur around a new excavated pool — the Amrit Sarovar, 'pool of nectar' — and invites traders and craftsmen of all communities to settle. The city that grows around the water will become the spiritual centre of the Sikh world: Amritsar.

title: The Adi Granth Compiled
body: Guru Arjan completes Harmandir Sahib — built with doors on all four sides, open to all four castes and every direction of the world — and installs the Adi Granth within it. The scripture he compiles includes not only the Gurus but Hindu bhagats and Muslim saints: Kabir, Farid, Ravidas, Namdev. Baba Buddha becomes its first granthi.

title: Martyrdom of Guru Arjan
body: Guru Arjan is tortured to death in Lahore on the orders of Emperor Jahangir — made to sit on a hot iron plate while burning sand was poured over him. Tradition records his response: 'Tera kia meetha lagai' — sweet is Your will. He is the first Sikh martyr, and his death turns the community's history.

title: Miri-Piri and the Akal Takht
body: Guru Hargobind, son of the martyred Guru, girds two swords — miri for worldly authority, piri for spiritual — and builds the Akal Takht, the Throne of the Timeless, facing Harmandir Sahib. The message: the spirit and the defence of the vulnerable belong together. The community learns to ride and to bear arms in self-defence.

title: Guru Har Rai — The Healer Guru
body: Guru Har Rai keeps a hospital and herbarium, sends rare medicine to the ailing son of the emperor who persecuted his grandfather, and is remembered for his tenderness toward living things — tradition says he once wept for blossoms broken by his robe. Sikh environmentalists today mark Sikh Environment Day on his gurgaddi anniversary.

title: Guru Har Krishan — The Child Guru
body: Guru Har Krishan receives the guruship at five years old. During a smallpox epidemic in Delhi he tends the sick regardless of caste or creed until he contracts the disease himself and dies at eight. Delhi's Gurdwara Bangla Sahib stands where he served; its langar and free clinic continue his work today.

title: Martyrdom of Guru Tegh Bahadur
body: Kashmiri Pandits facing forced conversion appeal to Guru Tegh Bahadur. He goes to Delhi knowing the price, refuses to convert or perform miracles, and is beheaded in Chandni Chowk. He gave his life for the religious freedom of a faith that was not his own — a martyrdom almost without parallel, and the deepest root of Sikh pluralism.

title: Vaisakhi — The Birth of the Khalsa
body: At Anandpur, Guru Gobind Singh asks the assembled thousands for a head. Five rise — a shopkeeper, a farmer, a tailor, a water-carrier, a barber, from five castes and five regions. These Panj Pyare receive amrit and the Guru then kneels to receive it from them. The Khalsa is born: Singh and Kaur, sovereign names that erase caste at a stroke.

title: Anandpur, Chamkaur, and Sirhind
body: Besieged at Anandpur and betrayed under safe conduct, the Guru's family is scattered. His two elder sons fall fighting at Chamkaur; the two younger — aged about six and nine — refuse conversion and are bricked alive and executed at Sirhind, where their grandmother Mata Gujri also dies. Sikhs remember the Sahibzade every December in the week called the Shaheedi days.

title: Mai Bhago and the Forty Liberated
body: Forty Sikhs who had deserted the siege of Anandpur are shamed and led back to battle by a woman, Mai Bhago of Jhabal. At Muktsar they fall defending the Guru, who tears up their letter of desertion and names them the Chali Mukte — the Forty Liberated. Mai Bhago, wounded, becomes the Guru's bodyguard and is remembered as the Khalsa's first great woman warrior.

title: Guru Granth Sahib — The Eternal Guru
body: At Nanded, dying of an assassin's wound, Guru Gobind Singh ends the line of human Gurus. He bows before the Adi Granth and vests the guruship in it forever: Guru Granth (the word) and Guru Panth (the community). From this day, the Guru of the Sikhs is a book of songs in which Hindu weavers and Muslim saints share pages with kings of the spirit.

title: Banda Singh Bahadur Takes Sirhind
body: Banda Singh Bahadur, the ascetic transformed by Guru Gobind Singh's touch, marches north and defeats the imperial army at Chappar Chiri, taking Sirhind — where the young Sahibzade were executed. He strikes coins in the name of the Gurus and enacts a revolution: land deeds to the peasants who till the soil. It is among the first land reforms in modern South Asian history.

title: Martyrdom of Banda Singh Bahadur
body: Captured after a long siege, Banda Singh Bahadur is paraded through Delhi in a cage and executed with hundreds of his companions, who are offered their lives for conversion and refuse, day after day, in public view. Contemporary Mughal and European accounts record their astonishing composure.

title: The Price on Every Sikh Head
body: Imperial decrees set rewards for the heads of Sikhs; the community is hunted into the forests and hills of Punjab. These are the decades of the horse's saddle as home — and of survival through the institutions Guru Gobind Singh left: the Sarbat Khalsa assembly and decisions taken as gurmatta, collectively, before the Guru Granth Sahib.

title: Nawab Kapur Singh and the Dal Khalsa
body: Nawab Kapur Singh — offered a title by the governor of Lahore, he accepted it only after the sangat's permission and kept fanning the congregation as before — organizes the scattered bands into the Dal Khalsa, with the Budha Dal to protect shrines and train, and the Taruna Dal to fight. Discipline, humility, and structure carry the Panth through annihilation campaigns.

title: The Chhota Ghallughara
body: The 'lesser holocaust': a Lahore-led campaign traps and kills an estimated seven thousand or more Sikhs along the Ravi. It is remembered as the first of two organized attempts in a single generation to erase the community entirely.

title: Baba Deep Singh's March to Amritsar
body: After Afghan forces demolish and defile Harmandir Sahib, the elderly scholar Baba Deep Singh — head of the Damdami Taksal, transcriber of the scripture — vows to reach the sarovar or die. Mortally wounded on the way, tradition holds he fought on to the precincts of the shrine. His memory stands for the union of scholarship and sacrifice.

title: The Wadda Ghallughara
body: Ahmad Shah Durrani's army falls upon the Sikhs' moving baggage train — mostly families — near Kup. In a single day an estimated twenty to thirty thousand are killed, perhaps a third of the entire community. Months later, the survivors defeat Durrani's forces in open battle. The Ghallughara is mourned; the recovery after it is the proof-text of Chardi Kala.

title: Lahore Taken, Harmandir Rebuilt
body: Within three years of the great killing, the misls take Lahore and begin rebuilding Harmandir Sahib from the rubble Durrani left. The pattern — destruction answered by reconstruction, not by despair — repeats so often in Sikh history that the rebuilt shrine itself becomes a doctrine in brick and gold.

title: The Twelve Misls
body: Punjab passes under twelve Sikh confederacies — the misls — each led by elected chiefs, cooperating (and quarrelling) through the Sarbat Khalsa. Under the rakhi system, villages of any faith pay a protective levy far lighter than imperial taxation. It is a rough, plural, surprisingly consultative order — a republic of horsemen.

title: The Khalsa at the Red Fort
body: Jassa Singh Ahluwalia, Jassa Singh Ramgarhia, and Baghel Singh ride into Delhi itself. Rather than hold the imperial capital, Baghel Singh negotiates the right to build gurdwaras at the sites of Sikh memory — including Sis Ganj, where Guru Tegh Bahadur was martyred. The shrines of Delhi date from this bargain: memory chosen over territory.

title: Ranjit Singh Takes Lahore
body: Nineteen-year-old Ranjit Singh of the Sukerchakia misl takes Lahore, ending the era of the warring confederacies. Two years later he is proclaimed Maharaja of Punjab — but he strikes coins not in his own name, instead in the name of the Gurus: the state is called Sarkar-e-Khalsa, and sovereignty is held in trust.

title: The Sarkar-e-Khalsa
body: Ranjit Singh's empire stretches from the Khyber Pass to the Sutlej. His court and army are deliberately plural — Muslim artillery generals, Hindu ministers, European officers; the Fakir brothers of Lahore among his closest advisors. Capital punishment is almost never used. He gilds Harmandir Sahib, giving the world the name 'Golden Temple,' and funds mosques and temples alike.

title: After the Lion — The Unravelling
body: Ranjit Singh dies in 1839 and the empire consumes itself: four rulers in six years, court factions, assassinations, an army grown beyond civilian control. Maharani Jind Kaur, regent for the boy-king Duleep Singh, struggles to hold the state together as the East India Company masses troops on the frontier.

title: The Anglo-Sikh Wars and Annexation
body: Two wars — Sobraon, Chillianwala, Gujrat — end the last major independent power in India. Punjab is annexed in 1849. The boy Maharaja Duleep Singh is separated from his mother, converted, and exiled to England; the Koh-i-Noor diamond is taken to the British crown. Jind Kaur escapes imprisonment to Nepal; mother and son meet again only after thirteen years.

title: The Singh Sabha Movement
body: Facing missionary pressure and the erosion of Sikh institutions, reformers found the Singh Sabha in Amritsar, then Lahore. Schools, printing presses, scholarship: a renaissance of Sikh learning that produces Khalsa College (1892), the scholar-poet Bhai Vir Singh, and the intellectual foundations of the modern Panth.

title: The Panth Crosses the Oceans
body: Sikh soldiers and farmers begin migrating to East Africa, Malaya, Canada, and California. The first North American gurdwaras open — Vancouver in 1908, Stockton in 1912 — and become anchors for communities that will one day number in the millions. The diaspora is born carrying langar with it.

title: The Ghadar Party
body: Punjabi immigrants in San Francisco — many of them Sikh farmers and ex-soldiers — found the Ghadar ('Mutiny') Party, publishing revolutionary newspapers in several languages and sailing home by the hundreds in 1914–15 to overthrow colonial rule. The rising fails; dozens are hanged, including nineteen-year-old Kartar Singh Sarabha, whom Bhagat Singh would name as his hero.

title: The Komagata Maru
body: Gurdit Singh charters a ship to challenge Canada's exclusion laws, carrying 376 passengers, mostly Sikhs, to Vancouver. They are held in harbour for two months and turned away; on return to India, twenty are shot dead at Budge Budge. Canada formally apologized in 2016. The voyage remains a touchstone for migrant justice.

title: Jallianwala Bagh
body: On Vaisakhi day in Amritsar, Brigadier Dyer orders troops to fire without warning on an unarmed gathering in the walled garden of Jallianwala Bagh, yards from Harmandir Sahib. Hundreds are killed, over a thousand wounded. The massacre radicalizes a generation across India. Twenty-one years later, Udham Singh — orphan, survivor — shoots the governor who approved it, in London.

title: The Gurdwara Reform Movement
body: To recover shrines from hereditary mahants backed by colonial power, Sikhs launch one of history's great nonviolent campaigns. At Nankana Sahib in 1921, over a hundred reformers are massacred; at Guru ka Bagh in 1922, wave after wave of Akalis take beatings without raising a hand, until even hardened observers weep. The Sikh Gurdwaras Act of 1925 places the shrines under the elected SGPC.

title: The Line Through Panjab
body: Independence comes with a border drawn through the middle of Sikh homeland. Punjab burns: up to a million people of all communities die in the violence; some fifteen million are displaced. Sikhs lose Nankana Sahib, Kartarpur, Panja Sahib — the birthplace and resting place of Guru Nanak — to the far side of a hard border. Nearly every Sikh family carries Partition in its memory.

title: Rebuilding in East Punjab
body: Refugee families rebuild from nothing — new farms on allotted land, new towns, new universities. The community that arrived with bullock carts becomes, within a generation, the engine of India's agriculture. The resilience is real; so are the wounds, spoken of rarely and carried long.

title: Punjabi Suba
body: After two decades of campaigning led by the Akali movement, Punjab is reorganized as a Punjabi-speaking state — the only Indian state with a Sikh majority. The settlement leaves disputes unresolved: Chandigarh as shared capital, river waters, and the division of Punjabi-speaking areas remain contested politics to this day.

title: The Green Revolution
body: Punjab's farmers adopt high-yield wheat and rice and feed a famine-threatened India — less than 2% of India's land producing a vast share of its grain reserves. The triumph carries a delayed bill: falling water tables, soil exhaustion, pesticide harm, and a debt cycle that will define rural Punjab's crisis half a century later.

title: The Anandpur Sahib Resolution and Rising Tensions
body: The Akali Dal's Anandpur Sahib Resolution demands greater federal autonomy for Punjab and redress on water, territory, and Sikh concerns. Through the late 1970s and early 1980s, political deadlock, the 1978 Nirankari clash, and escalating cycles of militancy and state response carry Punjab toward catastrophe.

title: Operation Blue Star
body: The Indian Army assaults the Harmandir Sahib complex to remove Jarnail Singh Bhindranwale and armed militants fortified inside. The attack falls on the anniversary of Guru Arjan's martyrdom, with thousands of pilgrims present. The Akal Takht is reduced to rubble; the reference library burns; official and independent casualty counts diverge widely, with many pilgrims among the dead. For Sikhs worldwide it remains an open wound, mourned every June.

title: The November Pogroms
body: After Prime Minister Indira Gandhi is assassinated by two Sikh bodyguards, organized mobs — with documented political direction and police inaction — kill more than three thousand Sikhs in Delhi alone, and thousands more across India. Voter lists were used to mark homes. Decades later, only a handful of convictions have been secured; for the survivors' colonies of Delhi, justice remains overdue.

title: The Dark Decade
body: Punjab passes through insurgency and counterinsurgency: militant violence against civilians, and state disappearances, custodial killings, and mass secret cremations documented by human-rights defenders. Jaswant Singh Khalra, who uncovered thousands of illegal cremations from municipal firewood records, is abducted and murdered in 1995. His words — 'I challenged the darkness; if nothing else, around myself, I will not let it settle' — are recited like scripture.

title: Kar Seva — The Akal Takht Rebuilt
body: The Akal Takht is rebuilt — first by government contract, which the community tears down as tainted, and then again by kar seva: voluntary labour, brick by brick, paid for by sangat donations alone. The act restates an old principle: what belongs to the Panth must be healed by the Panth's own hands.

title: Three Hundred Years of the Khalsa
body: The tercentenary of Vaisakhi 1699 is marked from Anandpur Sahib to gurdwaras on every continent. The community that a century earlier was counted in one province now spans some 25–30 million people worldwide — the world's fifth-largest organized religion, still anchored to the langar floor.

title: Khalsa Aid — Seva Without Borders
body: Ravi Singh founds Khalsa Aid on a single line of the ardaas — sarbat da bhala, the welfare of all. Its volunteers turn up with food, water, and shelter in Kosovo, Haiti, Syria, Yemen, Ukraine — wherever the need is, for whoever needs it. It becomes the most visible modern face of an old idea: the langar travels.

title: After September 11
body: Four days after 9/11, Balbir Singh Sodhi, a turbaned Sikh gas-station owner in Arizona, is murdered in the first of a wave of misdirected hate crimes. The community answers with education, not retreat: the Sikh Coalition, SALDEF, and others are founded; Sikhs win the right to serve in armed forces and police in their dastaars. Representation stops being optional and becomes seva.

title: EcoSikh and the Greening of the Panth
body: Sikh organizations found EcoSikh, declaring March 14 — Guru Har Rai's gurgaddi day — Sikh Environment Day, now marked by thousands of gurdwaras. Its Guru Nanak Sacred Forest project plants dense native micro-forests across Punjab and beyond, answering the Green Revolution's ecological debt with the oldest Sikh text of all: air the Guru, water the father, earth the great mother.

title: Oak Creek
body: A white supremacist murders six worshippers (a seventh dies of his wounds in 2020) at the gurdwara in Oak Creek, Wisconsin. Lt. Brian Murphy, shot fifteen times defending them, survives. The community's response — public grief, public forgiveness, and a langar opened wider than before — becomes a case study in answering hate without imitating it.

title: The Kartarpur Corridor
body: For Guru Nanak's 550th birth anniversary, India and Pakistan open a visa-free corridor to Kartarpur Sahib, where the Guru farmed and died. Pilgrims who for seventy years could only view the shrine through binoculars across the border now walk there. A border crossed by pilgrimage, on the anniversary of the man who said there is no Hindu, no Musalman.

title: The Farmers' Protest
body: Hundreds of thousands of farmers — Punjab's Sikhs prominent among them — encamp at Delhi's borders for over a year against new farm laws, running what observers call the world's largest langar: free meals daily for protesters, police, journalists, and passers-by alike. The laws are repealed in November 2021. The protest re-teaches a global audience what seva, sangat, and morcha mean.

title: The Work Continues
body: Sikhs today farm, code, govern, drive, heal, and serve on every inhabited continent. The questions of this century — ecological repair, migration, pluralism, justice — are questions the tradition has rehearsed for five hundred years. The chronicle stays open on purpose: in Chardi Kala, the rising is the point.
